text: removing a processor or heatsink identify the heatsink and processor socket components. before you perform this procedure, make sure that you have the following items available: this procedure shows the standard heatsink, but the steps apply to all heatsinks: to prevent possible server overheating, always populate each socket with a socket cover or blank or a processor and heatsink. power down the server. remove all power: disconnect each power cord from the power source. disconnect each power cord from the server. disconnect all peripheral cables from the server. remove the server from the rack remove the air baffle and any components that block access to the heatsink and processor. remove the fan cage. before continuing, allow all internal components to cool. unlatch the heatsink from the bolster plate. remove the processor heatsink assembly. separate the processor from the carrier and heatsink. using an alcohol wipe, clean the thermal grease from the processor and heatsink. the removal is complete. to replace the component, see installing a processor or heatsink .

source_file: HPE_sd00005862en_us_HPE ProLiant Compute DL380 Gen12 Server Maintenance and Service Guide.pdf
text: installing a processor or heatsink identify the heatsink and processor socket components. before you perform this procedure, make sure that you have the following items available: this procedure shows the standard heatsink, but the steps apply to all heatsinks: to prevent possible server overheating, always populate each socket with a socket cover or blank or a processor and heatsink. power down the server. remove all power: disconnect each power cord from the power source. disconnect each power cord from the server. disconnect all peripheral cables from the server. remove the server from the rack remove the air baffle and any components that block access to the heatsink and processor. remove the fan cage. verify that the processor is secure in the carrier. the processors and carriers are keyed. different carriers have different tabs in different locations. verify that the pin 1 indicators are aligned. verity that the processor is secured in the carrier. to prevent mechanical damage or depositing oil on your hands or other contaminants to the heatsink contact surface, hold the heatsink only by the edge of its

source_file: HPE_sd00005862en_us_HPE ProLiant Compute DL380 Gen12 Server Maintenance and Service Guide.pdf
text: 1 indicators are aligned. verity that the processor is secured in the carrier. to prevent mechanical damage or depositing oil on your hands or other contaminants to the heatsink contact surface, hold the heatsink only by the edge of its base plate. do not touch the heatsink fins. if you are using a new heatsink, remove the protective film from the thermal interface material. if you are reusing the same heatsink, verify that the old grease has been cleaned off of the processor. using the grease syringes, apply grease to the top of the processor. locate the pin 1 indicators on the carrier and heatsink. lower the heatsink on the processor carrier until the carrier latches into place. there will be an audible click to indicate that the heatsink is properly latched on the processor carrier. verify that the latches are secured to the heatsink. to prevent thermal failure or component damage, do not move the heatsink once the bottom of its base plate touches the top of the processor. excessive heatsink movement can cause the thermal grease to smear

section_path: reconfiguring the system date and time settings
text: reconfiguring the system date and time settings access the uefi system utilities. during post, press f9 . from the system utilities screen, select system configuration > bios/platform configuration (rbsu) > date and time . select a setting, and then complete your entry. date (mm-dd-yyyy) -enter the date in a month-day-year (mm-dd-yyyy) format. hour format -select either a 12- or 24-hours format. coordinated universal time (utc) -calculates the time stored in the hardware real-time clock (rtc) from the associated time zone setting. local time -removes the use of the time zone setting. this option is useful for addressing interaction issues in windows operating systems set in legacy bios boot mode. enabled -adjusts the local time displayed by one hour for daylight savings time. disabled -does not adjust the local time displayed for daylight savings time. to confirm and save the settings, press f12 .

text: remove all power: disconnect each power cord from the power source. disconnect each power cord from the server. disconnect all peripheral cables from the server. remove the server from the rack. place the server on a flat, level work surface. look for any potential contact between the coolant and any of the internal cables and components, especially power connectors. cover the spill point with a dry absorbent material. remove the leaky liquid cooling module. avoid pushing out more coolant during removal. remove the system board. if the leak has made it to the system board or the chassis, do the following: lightly dampen a cleanroom wipe with deionized water. wring out any excess water, and gently wipe over areas with coolant residue. ensure that there is no more visible colored coolant or liquid residue. dry the system board in a 70°c environment for at least 8 hours. confirm that the system board is completely dry before reinstallation. follow the procedure in the server maintenance and service guide to reinstall the system board. repeat steps 11-18 on other servers affected by

text: water and electricity combined pose a significant safety hazard. hardware electrical components that have been in contact with the spilled coolant might be damaged. to ensure a functional and safe server operation, identify and replace all damaged components. 24. after the spill is properly cleaned up, do not rush to power on the system immediately. instead, leave the system in standby mode first and observe the front panel leds after connecting the power cables. verify if the system power led is illuminated. if not, replace the system board. in the ilo web interface or restful api, clear the coolant leakage status. for the detailed procedure, see the ilo 7 user guide ( https:/ /www.hpe.com/support/hpeilodocsquicklinks ). if the server uses a dlc cold plate module, reconnect the dlc hoses to the rack manifolds. power on the server. if the system fails to boot, replace the system board.

text: removing and replacing the system battery the system battery provides power to the real-time clock. if the server no longer automatically displays the correct date and time, you might need to replace the system battery. the computer contains an internal lithium manganese dioxide, a vanadium pentoxide, or an alkaline battery pack. a risk of fire and burns exists if the battery pack is not properly handled. to reduce the risk of personal injury: do not expose the battery to temperatures higher than 60°c (140°f). do not expose the battery to extremely low air pressure as it might lead to explosion or leakage of flammable liquid or gas. do not disassemble, crush, puncture, short external contacts, or dispose of in fire or water. replace only with the spare designated for this product. power down the server. remove all power: disconnect each power cord from the power source. disconnect each power cord from the server. extend the server from the rack. remove the server from the rack. remove the battery. to replace the component, reverse the removal procedure. for more information about

text: front sff drives with a multipurpose cage certain components are color-coded. these colors represent the recommended touch areas for a removal process and indicate whether components require a system shutdown before removal. the following diagrams are examples only. hot-plug red indicates hot-pluggable components. these components can be removed and installed while the system is running, and doing so will not result in a system shutdown. power supplies in a redundant power configuration touchpoint blue indicates cold-pluggable components. these components require a system shutdown. failure to do so might result in system failure or data loss. cold-pluggable components might also indicate touchpoints on non-electrical components. the hpe basic drive carrier has the following leds: amber/blue led-managed by the drive backplane in conjunction with the storage controller and is used to indicate drive status. green led-managed by the drive itself and indicates the drive activity. the lff low-profile drive carrier supports hot-plug sas or sata. | item | led | state | definition | |:-------|:----------------|:-----------------------------------------|:------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------| | 1 | fault/locate | solid amber | this drive has failed, is unsupported, or is invalid.

text: of the cable. use the internal cable management features to properly route and secure the cables. route cables so that they do not contact or rest on cooling components, including heatsinks. ensure that cable routing does not obstruct airflow to or from heatsinks or ventilation openings when routing cables, be sure that the cables are not in a position where they can be pinched or crimped. avoid tight bend radii to prevent damaging the internal wires of a power cord or a server cable. never bend power cords and server cables tight enough to cause a crease in the sheathing. make sure that the excess length of cables is properly secured to avoid excess bends, interference issues, and airflow restriction. before installing a new component or closing up the server, make sure that all cables are in their appropriate routing position. this cable check prevents component damage and potential signal interference. grip the body of the cable connector. do not pull on the cable itself because this action can damage the internal wires of the cable or the pins on
